narrative:
Let's take a closer look at the prediction that your application for university admission was rejected because the model predicted you might fail the bar exam. This prediction is based on several factors from your academic and personal background. Currently, the model estimates a 55% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This decision was influenced by your academic performance, particularly your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, your LSAT score, and your undergraduate GPA, all of which are below the average of students who typically pass.

Counterfactuals are essentially "what if" scenarios that help us understand what changes could lead to a different prediction. They show us the smallest adjustments needed in your profile to flip the prediction from failing to passing. In your case, the model generated several counterfactual scenarios, each suggesting different combinations of changes that could improve your chances.

Across these scenarios, certain features consistently needed to change. For instance, improving your undergraduate GPA was a common factor. In some scenarios, a slight increase in your LSAT score also contributed to a more favorable prediction. Interestingly, your family income and whether you work full-time or part-time did not need to change in any scenario, indicating these factors were less critical in this context.

Out of the counterfactuals generated, the most frequent changes involved your academic performance metrics, such as your GPA and LSAT score. These features often changed together, suggesting that a combination of improved grades and test scores could significantly impact the prediction.

To improve your chances of passing the bar exam and having your application approved, focusing on enhancing your academic performance would be beneficial. Specifically, aiming to increase your undergraduate GPA closer to the average of 3.21 and boosting your LSAT score towards the average of 36.70 could make a substantial difference. While these changes might require effort and time, they are realistic goals that could positively influence your future outcomes. Remember, these insights are meant to guide you in making informed decisions about your academic journey.
